world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Map-based navigation system allowing player to select destination and travel instantly via cheat code
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player can traverse Neverland freely once unlocked through cheat input

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Level completion gates progression; ending sequence viewable after correct cheat code entry
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Levels must be completed sequentially to advance story, OR cheat code allows instant map access

environmental_storytelling.example: Game based on 1991 Hook film; visual themes reflect Neverland, pirate ships, and Peter Pan narrative setting
Japanese version displays developer credits in katakana alongside English names; US/EU versions use English-only credits with text space constraints

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Must complete levels sequentially to advance through story
Can access map screen and teleport to any destination without level completion

notes: Source provides limited gameplay/visual documentation. Data focuses on meta-level structure (credits, cheat codes, regional differences) rather than granular visual communication systems. Game's visual manifestation of danger, faction identity, and world-state details are not documented in The Cutting Room Floor article. Recommend supplemental sources: gameplay footage analysis, ROM sprite sheet extraction, or developer interviews for comprehensive manifestation layer data.
sources_used: tcrf - Hook (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor
